graph_title: Number of countries with mechanisms in place to enhance policy coherence of sustainable development
indicator_name: Number of countries with mechanisms in place to enhance policy coherence of sustainable development
SDG_GOAL__GLOBAL: Goal 17: Strengthen the means of implementation and revitalize the Global Partnership for Sustainable Development
SDG_TARGET__GLOBAL: Target 17.14: Enhance policy coherence for sustainable development
SDG_INDICATOR__GLOBAL: Indicator 17.14.1: Number of countries with mechanisms in place to enhance policy coherence of sustainable development
SDG_SERIES_DESCR__GLOBAL: SG_CPA_SDEVP - Mechanisms in place to enhance policy coherence for sustainable development (Percent) [17.14.1 ]
SDG_CUSTODIAN_AGENCIES__GLOBAL:
United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP)

The Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD)
STAT_CONC_DEF__GLOBAL:
• Definition

Policy Coherence for Sustainable Development[1] (PCSD) is an approach to integrate the dimensions of sustainable development throughout domestic, international and systemic policy-making. Its objectives in the context of the 2030 Agenda are to advance the integrated implementation of the 2030 Agenda by: (i) Fostering synergies and maximising benefits across economic, social and environmental policy areas and institutional frameworks; (ii) Balancing domestic policy objectives and institutional frameworks with internationally recognised sustainable development goals and other international agreements/frameworks; and (iii) Addressing the transboundary and long-term impacts of policies, including those likely to affect developing countries.

Concepts:

Mechanisms to enhance policy coherence can vary greatly from country to country and can take different forms. However, based on existing practice, it is possible to identify a range of mechanisms, which are likely to enhance policy coherence for sustainable development, and therefore the presence of a combination of these mechanisms in a given country would indicate progress towards meeting SDG indicator 17.14.1.

The United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP) and the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) have developed a composite indicator framework for SDG indicator 17.14.1 that is based on initial research on existing work, literature, partners and existing indicators on similar issues, as well as on country practices and experiences. This indicator considers institutional and organisational mechanisms that can support efforts in enhancing Policy Coherence for Sustainable Development (PCSD) across eight themes:

1. Political Commitment

2. Long-term Vision

3. The Integration of Sustainable Development into Policy and Finance

4. Whole-of-government Coordination

5. Sub-national Engagement

6. Stakeholder-engagement

7. Impact Assessment

8. Monitoring, Evaluation, and Reporting

1

Based on the OECD Recommendation on Policy Coherence for Sustainable Development. ↑

INST_MANDATE__GLOBAL: The United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP) and the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) were mandated as Custodian Agencies for indicator 17.14.1 by the Inter-agency and Expert Group on SDG Indicators and supports all work aspect in relation to Policy Coherence for Sustainable Development.
RATIONALE__GLOBAL:
Enhancing policy coherence for sustainable development is important for achieving sustainable development in its three dimensions (economic, social and environmental) in a balanced and integrated manner; for ensuring coherence between policies at various levels of government; and for ensuring that policies in different sectors are mutually supportive and do not work against each other. It is also important in addressing the transboundary impacts[2] of domestic policies, including their effects on other countries, and for anticipating long-term impacts on sustainable development outcomes over time.

Policy coherence aims, at minimum, to identify trade-offs and mitigate negative impacts between policies. At a more ambitious level, it should also aim to foster synergies and promote mutually reinforcing policies, ensuring they are implementable and sustainable. Achieving this requires that policies reflect and integrate the perspectives of the concerned stakeholders’ perspectives, thereby enhancing legitimacy, ownership, and long-term effectiveness.

2

Any effect – intended or not – originated in one country that crosses national borders through flows of capital, goods, human and natural resources, and that is able to affect positively or negatively the sustainable development prospects of another country. ↑
REC_USE_LIM__GLOBAL:
This methodology is based on a recognition that there is no one-size-fits-all approach to PCSD. Actions undertaken by countries to enhance policy coherence instead need to be adapted to their national realities, capacities, levels of development, territorial context, and other constraints through place-based policies and institutional structures[3]. As a result, the scoring system has been designed to recognise a broad range of institutional and organisational mechanisms as valid approaches to enhance policy coherence for sustainable development. This was done by providing detailed scoring guidelines, supported by illustrative examples, rather than a prescriptive set of criteria for each mechanism under assessment.

Equally, existing evidence and practice on PCSD demonstrates that it is essential to assess the level of implementation of each institutional or organisational mechanism in place to enhance policy coherence for sustainable development[4]. Policy coherence is a means to achieve sustainable development, not an end in itself. Therefore, the effectiveness of any mechanism depends on factors such as how systematically it is applied, its mandate to address policy trade-offs, and its level of enforceability. To address this, a ‘1 to 4’ scoring system was introduced to capture the degree of implementation of each mechanism under assessment. Detailed scoring guidelines were developed to help countries to determine which score best reflects the implementation level of their institutional or organisational mechanisms.

Detailed guidance, supported by data validation, is in place to address the qualitative features of this indicator, as described above. However, the flexible nature of the scoring system is still expected to limit the comparability of results between countries, and this must be acknowledged in reporting on SDG indicator 17.14.1. However, this methodology aims primarily to provide a basis for countries to engage in discussions around what policy coherence means at the national level and how it could be improved. Such discussions and strategies to improve policy coherence that may result from it could feed into a country Voluntary National Review (VNR) or National Development Strategy or Plan development, to inform further efforts by the country to improve its ability to implement the 2030 Agenda through better policy coherence.

This document should be considered a living document, regularly updated to reflect country experiences in establishing and assessing mechanisms for policy coherence. These experiences, and related challenges, lessons learned and solutions, can be shared so that UNEP and the OECD as custodian agencies, together with partners, can continue to refine this methodology and disseminate it not only as a tool to enable effective reporting but also to support national efforts toward policy coherence.

3

OECD, Recommendation of the Council on Policy Coherence for Sustainable Development, OECD/LEGAL/0381 ↑

4

OECD (2024), Unleashing Policy Coherence to Achieve the SDGs: An Assessment of Governance Mechanisms, OECD Publishing, Paris, https://doi.org/10.1787/a1c8dbf8-en ↑
DATA_COMP__GLOBAL:
The United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP) and the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) have developed a composite indicator framework for SDG indicator 17.14.1 that is based on initial research on existing work, literature, partners and existing indicators on similar issues. This indicator considers institutional and organisational mechanisms that can support efforts in enhancing Policy Coherence of Sustainable Development (PCSD) across eight themes:

1. Political Commitment

2. Long-term Vision

3. The Integration of Sustainable Development into Policy and Finance

4. Whole-of-government Coordination

5. Sub-national Engagement

6. Stakeholder-engagement

7. Impact Assessment

8. Monitoring, Evaluation, and Reporting

Each of the eight themes is scored on a scale from 0 to 12, where 0 means none of the requested mechanisms are implemented, 12 means all the requested mechanisms are in place and fully implemented. Scaling all 8 themes, the maximum value is 96.

Each theme includes three questions on mechanisms in place, scored on a scale from 0 to 4, indicating the level of implementation of the mechanism specified in the question. A general description of the level of implementation and scoring is provided in Table 1.

Table 1: A general description of the level of implementation and scoring for mechanisms in place with breakdown per level of implementation

Level of implementation

Clarification

Scoring

Not in place

The mechanism does not exist, and there are no plans or actions to develop it.

0

Under Development

The mechanism does not exist yet; however, there are formal plans to develop it, or it is currently under development.

1

In place, not implemented

The mechanism exists but has not yet been implemented. For example, it may be inactive or activities are of very low relevance to contribute to meaningful progress.

2

In place, partially implemented

The mechanism is in place but only partially implemented. Some components may be missing, which are necessary to ensure the mechanism supports efforts to enhance policy coherence. There might be several reasons for this, including insufficient funding, capacity constraints, lack of specific mandates, normative gaps, regulatory burdens, bureaucratic lengthy processes, etc.

3

In place, fully implemented

The mechanism is in place and fully implemented. The mechanism under assessment is complete and relevant in all aspects.

4

The score out of 96 is converted to a percentage for the purpose of reporting SDG indicator 17.14.1:

Mechanisms in place to enhance policy coherence of sustainable development (%) =

= S u m   o f   s c o r e s   o f   l e v e l s   o f   i m p l e m e n t a t i o n   a c r o s s   e i g h t   t h e m e s 96   × 100 %

More details are provided in the UNEP/OECD Questionnaire on the mechanism in place to enhance policy coherence of sustainable development and the document “Methodology for SDG-indicator 17.14.1: Mechanisms in place to enhance policy coherence for sustainable development” (will be updated by early 2026).
DATA_VALIDATION__GLOBAL: The United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP) and the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) carry out data validation procedures and contact countries for clarification if needed.
IMPUTATION__GLOBAL: The United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP) and the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) do not make any imputation for missing values.
REG_AGG__GLOBAL: The data are aggregated at the sub-regional, regional and global levels. For the aggregation methods, please see: https://wesr.unep.org/media/docs/graphs/aggregation_methods.pdf.
DOC_METHOD__GLOBAL: The methodology for calculating this indicator and guiding the reporting process are available in the UNEP/OECD Questionnaire on the mechanism in place to enhance policy coherence of sustainable development and the document “Methodology for SDG-indicator 17.14.1: Mechanisms in place to enhance policy coherence of sustainable development” (will be updated by early 2026).
QUALITY_MGMNT__GLOBAL: Quality management is provided by the United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP) and the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD).
QUALITY_ASSURE__GLOBAL: Quality assurance is provided by the United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP) and the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) in cooperation with the countries that provide these data.
QUALITY_ASSMNT__GLOBAL: Quality assessment is provided by the United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP) and the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD).
COVERAGE__GLOBAL:
Data availability:

Data are available for all countries that provide country data to the UNEP/OECD Questionnaire on the mechanism in place to enhance policy coherence of sustainable development.

Time series:

The data sets presented in the SDG database presented according to country responses.

Disaggregation:

Not applicable
